title: Structure and physicochemistry of anodic oxide films on titanium and TA6V alloy
abstract:
SURFACE AND INTERFACE ANALYSIS
Surf. Interface Anal. 27, 629È637 (1999)
Structure and Physicochemistry of Anodic Oxide
Films on Titanium and TA6V Alloy
V. Zwilling,1 E. Darque-Ceretti,1,* A. Boutry-Forveille,2 D. David,3 M. Y. Perrin1 and
M. Aucouturier4
1 Ecole des Mines de Paris, CEMEF (UMR CNRS 7635), BP 207, F-06904 Sophia-Antipolis, France
2 CNRS-Bellevue, Service dÏanalyse ionique, 1 Pl. A. Briand, F-92205 Meudon Cedex, France
3 Universite de Technologie, LG2mS (UPRESA CNRS 6066), BP 20,529, F-60205 Compie` gne Cedex, France
4 Universite Paris-Sud, Lab. Me tallurgie (URA CNRS 1107), Baü t. 410, F-91405 Orsay Cedex, France
Anodization of titanium and its alloys is an important surface treatment, especially for adhesion applications, but is
not as well studied as for aluminium alloys. This paper deals with the morphological, structural and physi-
cochemical characterization of anodic oxide Ðlms grown on titanium and Ti–6Al–4V (TA6V) in chromic acid
solution without (CA) or with (CA/HF) hydroÑuoric acid addition. Several investigations methods are used: high-
resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM), reÑection high-energy electron di†raction (RHEED), x-ray
photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS), nuclear reaction analysis (NRA) and
wetting angle measurements. The occurrence and morphology of the nanoporous structure for CA/HF anodization
are described. The compact Ðlms grown in CA solution are amorphous and the porous Ðlms grown in the CA/HF
solution are partially crystalline. The thickness and morphology of the Ðlms are described and discussed as a
function of the anodizing conditions and of the composition of the underlying substrate. The composition of the Ðlm
appears to be
(with Ti/Al atomic ratio ¿5), with incorporation of Ñuorine from the solution in the
TiO2 + Al2O3
porous Ðlms and of small quantities of vanadium in the Ðlms that are grown. The speciÐc role played by the Cr(VI)
and F species on the Ðlm growth-and-dissolution formation process is discussed and a growth mechanism is
proposed. Copyright
1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
(
KEYWORDS: anodization; titanium alloy; porous anodic Ðlm; XPS; SIMS; HR-SEM; NRA
INTRODUCTION
Anodic oxidation is a commonly developed surface
treatment1h3 for several applications, such as corrosion
protection, aspect improvement, joining with polymers,
etc. The insulating or semi-conductive properties of
anodic oxide Ðlms on some metals (often named “valve
metalsÏ) are also used in the manufacture of electrical or
electronic devices such as capacitors. Depending on the
metal substrate and electrochemical forming conditions,
the anodic oxide Ðlms may exhibit a compact or a
porous structure. Each structure has its own advantages
for given applications (e.g. a compact structure is neces-
sary for electrical application). The formation of porous
oxides is considered an important surface preparation
to improve the environmental behaviour of metals such
as aluminium alloys,1,2 titanium alloys4 or stainless
steels.5
The present study is devoted to the characterization
of oxide Ðlms grown on pure titanium and a TiÈ6%AlÈ
4%V (TA6V) alloy by anodic oxidation in chromic acid
media, to assess the adhesive properties of titanium and
its alloys for polymer joining. A previous study6 has
* Correspondence to:
Ecole des Mines de Paris,
E. Darque-Ceretti,
CEMEF (UMR CNRS 7635), BP 207, F-06904 Sophia-Antipolis,
France. E-mail: ceretti=cemef.cma.fr.
shown that the structure of the Ðlms may be either
compact or porous, depending on the composition of
the electrolyte used for their growth. The purpose of
this paper is to describe their physicochemical features,
investigated at di†erent scales, from the extreme surface
to the bulk and from the nanometre size of the pores to
the macroscopic surface reactivity and composition.
These features have been investigated using the follow-
ing methods:
(1) High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-
SEM) and reÑection high-energy electron di†raction
(RHEED) for structural investigation.
(2) X-ray-induced photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS),
secondary
ion
mass
spectrometry
(SIMS)
and
nuclear reaction analysis (NRA) for the composition
and thickness.
(3) Wetting angle measurements for the extreme surface
reactivity.
Fundamental investigations on anodic oxide struc-
ture and physicochemistry up to now have been
devoted mainly to aluminium for porous Ðlms and to
some valve metals, such as Al, Ta, V and Ti, for
compact Ðlms. For the metals and electrolytes con-
sidered hereÈAl and Ti alloys in chromic acid mediaÈ
the present comparative knowledge may be summarized
as follows:
(1) Anodic oxides on aluminium substrates are porous7
and may exhibit a compact or a porous structure on
titanium substrates.4,8 These structures depend on

Accepted 5 January 1999

V. ZWILLING ET AL .

the electrolyte composition and anodization pro-
cedure.
(2) The presence of Ñuoride ions in the electrolyte is
necessary for the occurrence of the porous structure
on titanium8 and its alloys, but not for Al alloys.6,9
(3) The ultimate thickness of the Ðlms is usually much
larger for Al alloys than for Ti alloys.
(4) Incorporation of elements from the electrolyte is
often reported and studied for Al alloys and also for
Ti alloys.10
(5) The inÑuence of the substrate alloying elements and
their incorporation (eventually enrichment) and dis-
tribution in the Ðlm have been studied intensively
for Al alloys11 but not for Ti alloys.
(6) The crystallography of the anodic oxides grown in
chromic acid media is not established.
EXPERIMENTAL
Substrates
The substrate composition, thickness and microstruc-
ture are summarized in Table 1. Impurities such as C,
O, N and H are \ 200 ppm.
It is important to mention that the microstructure of
the TA6V alloy mainly studied is biphased and consti-
tutes an Al-rich a-phase (HCP) with elongated grains
(10 lm or more), together with a small V-rich b-phase
(BCC) with small grains (2 or 3 lm). The pure Ti has an
equiaxial a-phase structure (30 lm grain). Anodization
of pure Al (99.99%) specimens in chromic acid media
has also been studied.
The specimens (3 ] 1.5 cm2) are mechanically pol-
ished to 1/4 lm diamond paste. Before anodizing, the
specimens are ultrasonically rinsed with methyl ethyl
ketone solution for 10 min. This surface preparation
was chosen because electrolytic polishing proved to lead
to non-homogeneous anodic Ðlms.9 This is probably
due to surface chemical modiÐcation by the polishing
procedure.
Anodization procedure
Anodization was performed in chromic acid media with
(CA) or without (CA/HF) hydroÑuoric acid addition.
The electrolyte compositions are given in Table 2,
together with an indication of the most probable species
present
in
the
solution,
obtained
from
the
concentrationÈpH diagrams computed by Pourbaix.12
Two anodizing conditions were studied:
(1) Anodization was conducted under potentiostatic
conditions at room temperature in a two-electrode
cell (inter-electrode distance \ 4 cm). The standard
procedure is to increase the potential between the
specimen and a titanium cathode from 0 V up to the
Ðnal voltage of 5 or 10 V by Ðve equal steps of 1
min each. Zero volts means that the anode and the
cathode are short-circuited. The specimen is then
maintained at the Ðnal voltage for variable times
between 1 and 55 min (i.e. 6È60 min total time).
(2) Some experiments were performed by reaching the
Ðnal voltage directly (without intermediary steps).
The growth sometimes has been interrupted at the
beginning of the procedure (short times and/or low
voltages) in order to follow the evolution of the Ðlm
structure.
Characterization methods
The anodized or untreated specimens were observed by
optical and scanning electron microscopy. In the latter
case, a high-resolution (Ðeld-ion gun) scanning electron
microscope was used in order to visualize the nanopo-
rosity. ReÑection high-energy electron di†raction pat-
terns were obtained in a Philips transmission electron
microscope conÐgured for grazing electron di†raction
and operating at 120 kV. Extractive replicas of the
surface (carbon ] platinum coating, dissolution of the
substrate in a bromine solution) allowed morphological
and crystallographic characterizations of the porous
structure.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were per-
formed in a Riber instrument under Mg Ka irradiation
(1253.6 eV). Semi-quantitative analysis was conducted
by reconstruction of the XPS peaks in order to extract
their
di†erent
components
using
standard
semi-
quantitative analysis procedures.13 In-depth proÐles
were obtained by ionic erosion (Ar gun, 3 keV). The
potential shift of the spectra due to constant charging of
the samples is compensated by calibrating all energies
with regards to the C 1s binding energy of the adventi-
tious carbon at 284.5 eV.
Secondary ion mass spectrometry analyses of the
composition proÐles in the Ðlms were performed in a
Cameca IMS4F instrument under Cs` primary bom-
bardment (14.5 keV, current 20 nA) and collection of
negative secondary ions. The primary beam is rastered
over an area 250 ] 250 lm, and the analysed area is 60

lm diameter. Charging e†ects were minimized by an

Nuclear reaction analysis was used to analyse quanti-
tatively the oxygen content of the Ðlms, using the
nuclear reaction: 16O (d,p) 17O, Ðrst excited state.14 The
energy of the incoming deuterons is 900 keV and a ref-
In order to characterize the acidÈbase reactivity of
the substrate and Ðlm extreme surface, a wetting angle
measurement method was used. For a given surface, the
wetting angle of drops of aqueous solutions with
varying pH are measured. An analysis of the variation
of that wetting angle with pH allows an acidÈbasic
titration of the surface, in the Bro nsted deÐnition. The
procedure of such measurements is described in another
publication.15 It is important to ensure that the pH
domain selected for the drop solutions corresponds to
the domain of three-dimensional stability of the investi-
dimensional stability domain of the anodic oxide is
estimated to be 3 \ pH \ 13.16
On aluminium substrate, whatever the electrolyte, the
oxide is porous with a pore diameter \ 10 nm, even in
the CA solution. On Ti 40 and TA6V, as mentioned in
the introduction, only the anodic oxide Ðlms formed in
CA solution are compact, and the Ðlms grown in the
CA/HF solution are made up of an underlying compact
layer surmounted by a porous layer with columnar
The porosity of the anodic Ðlm on Ti 40 and TA6V is
of nanometre size and distributed on a honeycomb-like
net [Figs 1(a) and 1(b)]. The size (18È40 nm) and dis-
tribution of the nanoporosity at the end of the forming
procedure in CA/HF solution depend on the substrate
(Ti 40 or TA6V) and on the elaboration conditions
(voltage, total time), as detailed in another publication.6
Whatever the considered substrate, the highest is the
voltage and the largest are the pores. The pores are
smaller on titanium substrate [Fig. 1(a)] than on TA6V
alloy substrate [Fig. 1(b)], whatever the applied voltage.
On the TA6V alloy, the porosity diameter depends on
the nature of the underlying phase (a or b) [Figs 1(b)
and 1(c)]. The diameters of the pores are of smaller
dimension on top of the V-enriched b-phase than on
top of the a-phase (e.g. 15 nm on the b-phase and 25 nm
on the a-phase after anodization at 5 V). The pore mor-
phology is also evidenced from transmission electron
microscopy of extraction replicas (Fig. 2). Such obser-
vation allowed an estimation of the thickness of the
porous part of the oxide Ðlm. More detailed informa-
tion on the respective thickness of the compact and
porous part of the Ðlms is obtained from the SIMS
analyses and will be discussed in the next section.
Under both anodizing conditions studied, i.e. when
(procedure 2) and/or at low voltage (procedure 1), the
morphology of the Ðlm is completely di†erent (Fig. 3):
anodic oxide films grown on titanium and TA6V alloy substrates in
CA/HF solution. Note the difference between the underlying a-
and b-phases: (a) Ti, 5 V, 20 min; (b) TA6V, 5 V, 20 min; (c)
small spherical particles (\20 nm) are observed at a
very earlier step, before nucleation of the pores [Figs
3(a) and 3(b)]; in a second step for the TA6V alloy, the
biphased structure begins to be revealed [Fig. 3(c)]; the
spherical particles disappear progressively and the dif-
ference between the a- and b-phases of the TA6V alloy

of a C ½Pt replica from the anodic oxide on TA6V (10 V, 20 min).
The average deepness of the porosities is Á75 nm between the
is clear. The Ðnal porous or compact morphology is sta-
bilized after 20 min for both 5 and 10 V anodization
voltages. This formation of spherical particles is attrib-
uted to a preferential etching of the substrate during the
Ðrst step of the process, and should be related to the
presence of Cr(VI) species shown by XPS analysis (see
next section). The progressive di†erentiation between
the a- and the b-phases during the second step of the
growing process is attributed to the di†erence in com-
position between the two phases, and to the speciÐc role
of the vanadium element in the growing mechanism, as
deduced from the electrochemical investigation.9
Electron grazing di†raction diagrams performed on
the surfaces of the Ðlm growth on the TA6V alloy lead
(1) The compact Ðlms (grown in CA solution) are
amorphous, even for a high elaboration voltage (10
(2) The porous Ðlms (grown in CA/HF solution) are
partially crystalline with the
least on their surface (Fig. 4).
The fact that the structure of the compact Ðlm remains
amorphous for a high forming voltage is in disagree-
ment with observations by Leach,17 showing a crys-
tallization of anodic Ðlms for voltages higher than the
breakdown voltage. Indeed, in the present case, the
appearance of break-down Ðgures (pits) on the speci-
mens has shown that the breakdown voltage lies below
The initial surface of the substrate (before anodization)
was analysed by XPS, which reveals the presence of a
TA6V), probably hydrolysed. The presence of hydroxyl
groups is evidenced by the O 1s (Fig. 5) peak splitting
into two components, and conÐrms the results of a
recent investigation.18 The extreme surface is contami-
nated with carbon, eliminated by as short (a few
minutes) ionic erosion. The compositions of the anod-
ized Ðlms for the di†erent substrates and anodization
conditions are summarized in Table 3 (the hydrogen
content, not detectable by XPS, is omitted). It is shown
that the quantity of contaminating carbon is two or
three times smaller on the anodic Ðlms than on the
The surface composition of the anodic Ðlm does not
depend on the anodization voltage and time for high
voltage (5È10 V) and long times (more than a few
minutes). For short anodization times, corresponding to
the occurrence of the spherical particles mentioned in
the previous section, a signiÐcant amount of chromium
is present. The binding energy measured for this chro-
occurrence of Cr(VI) species, which indicates that the
chromium is directly incorporated from the chromate
solution. The incorporation of chromium disappears for
longer anodization times. Fluorine is always incorpor-
ated in the porous Ðlms grown in the CA/HF solution.
Apart from these contaminating elements, the overall
composition of the Ðlms corresponds to TiO2 ] Al2O3 ,
with Ti/Al \ 5. Vanadium is never detected in the
Secondary ion mass spectrometry analyses were used
for conÐrmation of the chemical composition of the
Ðlms, and also in order to measure, by proÐlometry of
Figure 6 shows, respectively, the secondary ion proÐles
measured for a compact [Fig. 6(a)] and a porous [Fig.
6(b)] Ðlm. Because the primary current density is the
same for both proÐles, it must be kept in mind that the

ical particles; (b) 5 V, 5 s, note the spherical particles; (c) 2 V, 2
min, note the differentiation of the a- and b-phases.
much thinner compact Ðlm is sputtered out within a
much shorter time (\200 s) than the porous Ðlm (600 s).
Negative ions with mass numbers 1(H~), 16(O~),
The contribution of vanadium hydride ions in the
mass 52 signal could not be separated from the chro-
mium ions. It is nevertheless important to underline the
Figure 4. Reflection high-energy electron diffraction patterns of
porous anodic oxide on TA6V alloy: (a) 5 V, 20 min; (b) 10 V, 60
variation of that signal inside the Ðlm: the Ðrst part,
corresponding to the extreme surface, is of a very low
level, conÐrming the absence of chromium. The signal
increases abruptly at 200 s sputtering time, and this
variation is associated with an increase of the hydrogen
(mass 1) signal, indicating that it should be attributed to
the presence of vanadium in the Ðlm at a level as high
as in the substrate. Such an interpretation was con-
Ðrmed by analyses conducted on anodic Ðlms on pure
titanium, for which the signal at mass 52 remains negli-
gible. The proÐles conÐrm also the presence of alu-
minium (mass 27) and the incorporation of Ñuorine
(mass 19) in the whole thickness of the Ðlm.
The proÐle of the signal of mass 64(TiO~) is used as
an indicator for localization of the interface between the
Ðlm and the substrate. Figure 7 shows the shape of that
proÐle for di†erent anodizing conditions. The sputtering
has been interrupted when the signal has fallen down to
10% of its plateau value, and the sputtering crater
depth was measured by proÐlometry. The results for
porous Ðlms grown on TA6V (through anodization pro-
cedure 1, beginning by a progressive increase of the
voltage) are given in Table 4. The thickness of the
compact Ðlms (in CA solution) could not be measured
because it falls within experimental error (10È20 nm).
From Table 4 it can be seen that the thickness increases
more rapidly with the total time than with the Ðnal
Copyright ( 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Surf. Interface Anal. 27, 629È637 (1999)
Figure 5. X-ray photoelectron spectra on native oxide of a
mechanically polished and ultrasonically rinsed (with methyl ethyl
ketone) TA6V substrate: (a) peak O 1s, sputtering time 0 min; (b)
peak O 1s, sputtering time 5 min; (c) peak Ti 2p, sputtering time 0
min; (d) peak Ti 2p, sputtering time 5 min.
Figure 6. Secondary ion mass spectrometry profiles on anodic
films: primary Cs½ ions, 14.5 keV, 20 nA, detection of negative
secondary ions (for species identification, see text). (a) Compact
film on TA6V in CA solution (10 V, 20 min). (b) Porous film on
TA6V in CA/HF solution (10 V, 20 min).
A careful examination of the proÐles of Fig. 7 shows

The compact anodic Ðlms grown on TA6V alloy in CA
Figure 11. Proposition of a growth mechanism.
increases with the voltage (5 nm V~1 for TA6V).
In CA/HF acid solutions, the anodic Ðlms constitute
a compact underlying layer surmounted by a layer with
columnar pores of nanometre size. Protrusions similar
treated in CA solution are evidenced for the Ðrst time.
The thickness of oxide Ðlms on titanium and TA6V sub-
strate is time- and voltage-dependent. On the TA6V
alloy, their surface composition is also TiO2 ] Al2O3
(with Ti/Al D 5) but a small amount of vanadium is
present. The Ðlms incorporate F~-containing species
from the solution throughout their thickness. The
surface is at least mostly crystalline with the
phase, undetected by RHEED dia-
grams, is supposed to be amorphous.
During the Ðrst stage of the growth, and only during
that stage, chromium is present at the surface as Cr(VI)
species, and small particles are present before the
appearance of the porous structure. The particles are
develops for anodization in Ñuoride-containing solu-
tion. The Cr(VI) species disappear also. From those
observations, and from a complete electrochemical
study,9 it is proposed that the growing and pore forma-
tion process obey a growthÈdissolution mechanism
monitored by poisonÈantidote competition, as proposed
recently in the literature for another system (randomly
porous structure on Li in SiOCl2)19
(1) A shown in Fig. 11, in a Ðrst step a non-porous
Cr(VI) species would play a poisoning role and the
growth of the compact Ðlm formed in CA media
(2) When the solution contains Ñuorine ions, these
would play an antidote role and the local com-
petition between Cr(VI) and F~ species leads to a
continuous growth of the Ðlm with a randomly
porous structure,9 due to the competition between
oxide formation and the metal and Ðlm dissolution.
The constancy of the oxygen content may be
thickening and pore extension due to competition
between dissolution and growth.9
On the TA6V alloy, the thickness of the Ðlm and the
size and distribution of the pores depend on the under-
lying substrate structure: the dissolution is slightly
faster on top of the vanadium-enriched b-phase than on
top of the a-phase. This reveals the inÑuence of the
alloying elements on the growth and dissolution mecha-
nism. From the electrochemical study already men-
tioned,9 it seems that vanadium plays a predominant
